Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.

Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.

When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.

When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.

Level 2: you have used 39 actions; a strong score needs about 33 or fewer. You are 1.2x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
OK so there's no white-bordered 12-pixel object now. All four remaining objects are gray-bordered. The white border was removed when I clicked on the orange object.
Let me reconsider the game mechanics from scratch. Looking at what I know:
1. There are bordered objects (12 pixels each) that can be white-bordered or gray-bordered
2. There's an orange junction object (20 pixels)
3. There's a purple object (20 pixels) that moves
4. There are trail segments connecting objects
5. Clicking on trail segments moves the white border along the trail
6. Clicking on a bordered object (when white is elsewhere) moves the white border there and removes the previous white-bordered object
7. Clicking on the orange object removes the current white-bordered object (but only if white is at a certain position?)
Wait, let me reconsider. When I clicked on orange at turn 56 (white at 35,45), (35,45) was removed. When I clicked on orange at turn 59 (white at 11,34), (11,34) was NOT removed - it just became gray.
The difference might be the position of the purple object. At turn 56, the purple object was at (38,42). At turn 59, the purple object is at (35,38).
Or maybe the difference is the direction from which the white border approaches the orange object.
Actually, let me reconsider the entire game. Maybe the goal is not to remove all bordered objects, but to move the purple object to a specific position.
Looking at the purple object's movement:
- Initially at some position
- After clicking (28,37) at turn 56: moved to (35,38)
The purple object moved from (38,42) to (35,38), which is up and left.
Let me think about this differently. Maybe the game is about:
1. Moving the white border along the trail
2. The purple object follows the white border
3. The goal is to get the purple object to a specific position
Or maybe the bordered objects are obstacles that need to be removed, and the removal mechanic depends on the position of the white border relative to the orange object.
Let me try a different approach. Let me click on one of the remaining bordered objects directly to see if it removes another object.
Actually, I realize I need to first move the white border to one of the objects. Let me click on a trail segment to move the white border.
Since there's no white-bordered object now, I need to find a way to "reactivate" the white border. Let me click on a trail segment adjacent to one of the remaining objects.
Let me try clicking on (13,35) which is the trail segment adjacent to (11,34).

</parameter>
